sources.quote: you can still have your main accounts, your main organic accounts, pull all the views, but you can hire 50 VAs and repost on as many accounts as you like on this side

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm currently uses Amin (contractor) for manual posting (§5) and editors for production (§1). There is no VA layer for repurposing account management. Hiring/delegation infrastructure for this role does not exist yet (§10 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: When Palm is ready to scale social distribution, define a 'Repurposing VA' role with a clear SOP: download source, apply structural edits per checklist, assign to rotation slot, schedule via Publer. Start with one VA per 5 repurposing accounts. Document the SOP before hiring.

findings.title: Target mid-tier creators (10K followers, ~$1-3K/mo) not top earners

variants.claim: Beginner agencies should target creators with ~10-15K followers already making $1-3K/month rather than chasing large verified accounts who won't respond.
variants.what_they_do: Explicitly filter outreach to creators in the 10K-15K follower range earning roughly $1-3K/month on OF, on the basis that they are more open to support, more reachable via DM, and more likely to convert to a managed relationship.

sources.quote: targeting more beginner friendly creators as well. Also, someone that's kind of starting out, 10,000 15,000 followers, ones that are also making around 10K a month or so

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is Josh-DM-led with no documented targeting criteria (§6 GAP). No internal baseline exists for follower count, current earnings, or engagement thresholds that qualify a creator for outreach.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Codify a minimum viable creator profile for outreach: e.g., 10K-20K IG followers, $1-3K/mo existing OF revenue, active posting cadence. Add these as qualification fields to the creator onboarding form or a pre-onboarding screening step in Airtable.

findings.title: Start with exactly 2 clients — not 1, not 3+

variants.claim: New agencies should aim to sign two clients simultaneously: one as a hedge if the other underperforms, but no more than two to avoid operational overwhelm before systems are proven.
variants.what_they_do: Prescribes a deliberate two-client start: if one relationship fails the other provides continuity, while capping at two prevents the operator from over-extending before they have SOPs, chatters, or reliable traffic in place.

sources.quote: you should ideally aim to sign two clients from the get-go. If you just have one, you can stay with one, of course, but in most cases, one of them might not work out

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented guidance on optimal portfolio size at different stages of agency maturity (§6 GAP). No scaling model or client-count-to-capacity ratio is written down.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define explicit capacity tiers in Palm's onboarding/ops documentation: e.g., Tier 0 (founding) = 1-2 creators, Tier 1 = up to 5 once chatting is delegated, Tier 2 = 10+ once a full team is in place. Use this to gate new creator signings against current team bandwidth.

findings.title: Hard floor of 50/50 revenue split — never go lower

variants.claim: Agencies should refuse any deal below a 50% commission split, framing it as the clean, fair baseline rather than a negotiable starting point.
variants.what_they_do: States a non-negotiable 50/50 split as the minimum: if a creator earns $2K/month, the agency takes $1K. No tiered structures, performance bonuses, or sliding scales are mentioned — flat 50% is presented as the standard.

sources.quote: don't go below 50/50 revenue split. If your model makes $2,000 a month, that's 1,000 for her and 1,000 for you. Clean, fair, and simple.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm sets commission % per creator at onboarding but has no documented split rationale, floor, or tier model (§7 GAP). The commission field exists in the Creators table but the logic behind what percentage to set is unwritten.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document Palm's commission policy explicitly: establish a floor (50% is industry-standard per this and other sources), define whether Palm ever takes less (e.g., for a high-revenue creator where 50% would be disproportionate) and codify the rationale. Add this to the creator onboarding wizard as a displayed policy, not just a blank field.

findings.title: Start organic on Twitter, TikTok, IG only — add Reddit/bots later

variants.claim: New agencies should limit traffic sources to organic short-form content on Twitter, TikTok, and Instagram, deferring Reddit, bots, and shoutouts until the funnel is proven.
variants.what_they_do: Recommends a three-platform organic-only traffic strategy for beginners: push short-form video content on Twitter/X, TikTok, and Instagram. Explicitly deprioritizes Reddit, paid shoutouts, and automation tools as advanced tactics — not because they don't work, but because complexity at the start obscures what's actually driving results.

sources.quote: start simple things like Twitter, Tik Tok, Instagram, and that's it. Just push organic short form content. No need to over complicate it

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's traffic/distribution strategy is not documented (§5 GAP). Palm does run IG and social content production (§1) but has no written playbook for multi-platform distribution sequencing or when to layer in Reddit/paid channels.
palm_comparison.recommendation: For new creator onboarding, define a Phase 1 traffic stack (IG + TikTok organic, consistent with Palm's existing content pipeline) and a Phase 2 expansion trigger (e.g., once a creator hits X subs or Y MRR, add Reddit and X/Twitter). Document this as a traffic phase ladder so operators know when and how to escalate.

findings.title: Never mention OnlyFans by name on social — use clean indirect CTAs

variants.claim: Directly referencing OnlyFans on social media platforms (IG, TikTok) risks shadowbans or account termination, so all CTAs must be indirect and discreet.
variants.what_they_do: Instructs operators to keep the social-to-OF funnel link-based but never platform-name the destination in captions, bios phrasing, or spoken content. No five-step click chains either — the funnel is: social content → link → OF page, with the OF brand name omitted from the social layer.

sources.quote: do not mention only fans directly on social media because you are going to get banned. Shadowbanned or worse, right? Just don't do it.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content pipeline (§1) produces social posts but has no documented compliance rules around OF mention avoidance or shadowban risk on IG/TikTok. This is a platform-risk gap (§ platform-risk) that could silently harm creator accounts.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a compliance rule to Palm's content/posting SOP: no direct OF platform name mentions in captions, audio, or on-screen text on IG or TikTok. Standardize CTA language (e.g., 'link in bio,' 'exclusive content,' 'join my page') across all creator accounts. Include this as a checklist item in the editor approval workflow.

findings.title: Agency founder must chatter personally before hiring chatters

variants.claim: Before delegating DM monetization to hired chatters, the agency operator must handle messaging themselves to understand fan psychology and what drives spending.
variants.what_they_do: Requires the founder to personally reply to DMs in the early stage — not as a permanent task but as a learning phase. The stated goal is to internalize why fans message, why they send money, and what emotional triggers drive PPV conversions, before writing SOPs or hiring people to do it.

sources.quote: You need to understand the psychology. Why is this dude, you know, typing a message this girl online? Why is he here? Why is he sending money? You need to understand these things.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm currently outsources all chatting to an external team (§8) and has no internal SOPs, scripts, or fan psychology documentation. This means Palm's leadership may lack the ground-level insight needed to evaluate chatter quality or build in-house scripts for the roadmap in-house chat team.
palm_comparison.recommendation: As Palm moves toward an in-house chat team (~1yr roadmap, §8), leadership should run a time-boxed hands-on chatting sprint (e.g., 2-4 weeks on 1-2 accounts) to build firsthand understanding of fan psychology before writing scripts or hiring. Document findings as the foundation of Palm's internal chatting SOP.

findings.title: Hire chatters on flat rate, commission, or hybrid — reinvest first revenue

variants.claim: Once initial revenue is generated, the first reinvestment should be hiring chatters on a flat rate, small commission, or hybrid comp structure to free up operator time for scaling.
variants.what_they_do: Describes a reinvestment-first scaling loop: operator chats manually → earns first revenue → hires chatters (flat fee or commission or both) → reclaims time → signs more clients → hires more chatters → repeats. The comp structure is flexible (flat/commission/hybrid) rather than a fixed model.

sources.quote: you can then reinvest it and you can hire what are called chatters. These are people who handle all of the DMs for you, either for a flat rate or a small commission or a mixture of both.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is fully outsourced (§8) so this loop is bypassed at the current stage. However, Palm has no documented chatter comp model, hiring criteria, or onboarding process for when it builds an in-house team. This is a build-toward gap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Begin documenting chatter comp benchmarks now (flat rate ranges, commission % norms, hybrid structures) sourced from competitor content like this. Build a chatter job description and scoring rubric ahead of the in-house chat team launch. This prevents scrambling when the timeline moves up.

findings.title: Hold weekly video calls with each creator to give account updates and collect feedback

variants.claim: A recurring weekly Zoom/video call with the model — showing results, flagging problems honestly, and requesting content adjustments — is the primary relationship-maintenance mechanism.
variants.what_they_do: They schedule a standing weekly call per model on a video platform (Zoom or equivalent). The call covers: current account performance update (good and bad news), requested content changes for the coming week, and any operational issues. The visual face-to-face element is cited as relationship-strengthening beyond what async messaging achieves.

sources.quote: set up a Cadence of calls like on a weekly basis where you get on a zoom or get on some sort of call with the model you see them face to face and you explain what's going on

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented creator success cadence or QBR rhythm (§9 flags this as a gap). Communication is through Telegram per §5. There is no standing weekly call structure with creators.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Implement a standing weekly 20-min creator check-in call. Build a lightweight call template in Notion: (1) last-week metrics summary pulled from OF dashboard, (2) content requests for next week, (3) any platform/account issues, (4) open Q&A. Log call notes back to the Creators Airtable record. This directly fills the §9 retention/churn gap.

findings.title: Use weekly invoicing explicitly as a trust signal, not just a payment mechanism

variants.claim: Consistent invoicing builds model trust because it creates transparency and accountability, making it irrational for the model to leave a reliable agency over a single payment.
variants.what_they_do: They invoice every creator weekly and explain the invoicing schedule during the onboarding call. They frame invoicing not just as revenue collection but as a trust-building tool — a paper trail that shows the agency is accountable. They also argue that a model generating consistent income has no incentive to leave, reducing churn.

sources.quote: always invoice your clients it's a way to build trust but they're also not going to run if you provide a quality service

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm already auto-generates weekly invoices (§7 — multi-account PDF via Puppeteer, Dropbox, Resend). However, Palm's invoicing system is described as an ops tool, not a trust/relationship tool. There is no documented practice of *explaining* the invoicing schedule during onboarding or positioning it as a transparency signal to the creator.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add an 'invoicing walkthrough' step to the onboarding call checklist: show the creator the invoice format, confirm the payment cadence, and explicitly frame it as mutual accountability. This costs nothing — Palm's invoice infrastructure already exists — but closes the relationship-framing gap.

findings.title: Proactively disclose account issues to creators — hiding problems destroys trust faster than the problem itself

variants.claim: Agencies that hide negative account developments lose model trust catastrophically when the model discovers the issue independently; proactive disclosure, even of bad news, strengthens the relationship.
variants.what_they_do: Their agency policy is to give updates on account performance whether good or bad. If something goes wrong (traffic drop, platform issue, content underperformance), they inform the model directly rather than waiting or papering over it. This is presented as a cultural value established from onboarding onwards.

sources.quote: you don't want to just make it seem like everything's going perfectly and them to figure out something's wrong on their own because that's going to be just relationship destroying

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented creator communications policy around bad news or account issues (§6 thin, §9 no retention strategy). The weekly invoice cadence provides a financial signal but there is no SOP for flagging non-financial account problems to creators.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'bad news protocol' to Palm's creator comms SOP: define what events trigger a proactive creator notification (e.g., >20% subscriber drop, platform flag, traffic source failure) and set a response-time SLA (e.g., notify within 24 hours). Document this protocol and mention it during onboarding so creators know to expect it. Supports §9 retention.

findings.title: Use the onboarding process itself as a passive screening tool for creator quality and reliability

variants.claim: How a model responds during onboarding — responsiveness, receptiveness to feedback, implementation of early requests — is a leading indicator of her long-term revenue potential and whether the agency should continue investing in her.
variants.what_they_do: They observe model behavior during the onboarding phase as a quality signal: slow responses, failure to implement feedback, or resistance to criticism are treated as early warnings that the creator will not reach top-tier earnings. They acknowledge that new agencies must work with whoever they can get but advise building judgment over time to screen for quality clients.

sources.quote: if they're flaky or they don't respond very good or they take forever to respond or they don't Implement criticism you most likely don't have a very high quality client

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's onboarding is a form wizard (§6) — it captures data but generates no behavioral signal about the creator. There is no documented creator-quality scoring or early-stage filtering criteria.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 30-day post-onboarding creator quality review field to the Creators Airtable table. Score creators on: (1) content submission timeliness, (2) responsiveness on Telegram, (3) implementation of feedback from first weekly call. Use scores to prioritize agency resource allocation and, as the roster scales, to inform future signing decisions.

findings.title: Package VA chatter training as a zipped module with persona PDF

variants.claim: Sending a structured, zipped training module to new chatter VAs — including a persona guide, payment info, and scripts — accelerates onboarding and ensures consistency.
variants.what_they_do: When hiring a chatter VA, Bjorn compiles a ZIP package containing: (1) a PDF guide on the model's persona (age range, niche, typing style, pet names, emojis), (2) payment/platform instructions, (3) a pre-written script. The VA unzips and self-trains before starting.

sources.quote: some training material that I prepare for our VAs so when I hire a VA for the purposes of chatting I'll input the necessary information from the model in this guide here zip it up along with a few other things and then send it to the VA the chatter as a training module

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm currently outsources chatting to an external team (§8) with no documented internal SOP or onboarding material. There is no equivalent persona brief or chatter training kit. This is a direct gap to fill as Palm builds toward an in-house chat team.
palm_comparison.recommendation: As Palm builds its in-house chat team (~1yr roadmap), create a per-creator ZIP/Notion training module template: persona card (age, niche, tone, emojis, pet names), platform walkthrough, PPV pricing ladder, and a baseline script. Standardize this as the chatter onboarding artifact.

findings.title: Brief chatters on model persona: tone, emojis, typing quirks, niche

variants.claim: Chatters must mirror the model's real personality and typing style (excessive letters, cute emojis, pet names) so subscribers cannot detect a VA is behind the keyboard.
variants.what_they_do: The persona section of the training PDF specifies: age range of the model → inferred typing style (e.g. 18–24 = lots of cute emojis, elongated words like 'heyyyy'), the model's content niche, any pet names she uses, and other personality markers. Chatter applies all of these verbatim.

sources.quote: take note of the client's Persona so if the girl is aged between say 18 to 24 then using a lot of cute emojis when typing using a lot of excessive letters in some words

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is fully outsourced (§8) and there is no documented persona-briefing process internally. When Palm builds in-house or AI chatting, voice consistency will be critical — especially for AI personas (§3) where breaking character destroys the illusion.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'chatter voice card' field to the Creators table in Airtable (and AI Account Profile for AI personas). Populate: age, typing quirks, signature emojis, pet names, niche. Feed this card to any chatter (human or AI) at session start. Especially high priority for AI persona chatting.

findings.title: Price PPVs at .99 endings to exploit charm pricing psychology

variants.claim: Pricing PPV content at $X.99 instead of round numbers (e.g. $49.99 vs $50) makes content feel meaningfully cheaper and increases purchase rates.
variants.what_they_do: Any PPV intended to sell at a round number is repriced to one cent below: $50 → $49.99, $35 → $34.99. This is presented as a direct borrowing of supermarket/retail charm-pricing psychology and is codified as a standing rule in the chatter training guide.

sources.quote: avoid whole numbers when pricing PPVs so if you plan to sell a piece of content for 50 then you'll just reprice it and sell it for $49.99

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal PPV pricing model is documented at Palm (§7 GAP). There is no codified rule on round vs. charm pricing. This is a quick, zero-cost win to adopt.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a pricing rule to the future PPV pricing SOP: all PPV prices must end in .99 (never round numbers). Apply immediately when Palm's in-house chat team is stood up, and bake into any AI chatting prompt templates.

findings.title: Start subscribers on $15–$20 PPVs before escalating to $80–$100+

variants.claim: Sending low-ticket PPVs ($15–$20) first gauges a subscriber's willingness to spend and builds purchasing habit before high-ticket ($80–$100+) asks.
variants.what_they_do: Chatter begins by offering content in the $15–$20 range ('dipping a toe in the water'). After multiple purchases at this level, the chatter reads the subscriber's comfort and escalates to high-ticket PPVs ($80, $90, $100+). No high-ticket offer is sent cold to a new subscriber.

sources.quote: you'll start off selling subscriber content from $15 to $20 so it's kind of like dipping into the water seeing where they're at and then after the subscribers purchase multiple pieces you can start selling PPVs for high ticket prices like 80 bucks 90 100

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented PPV pricing ladder or sequencing rules (§7 GAP, §8 outsourced). The external chat team may do this implicitly, but it is not a formalized Palm playbook. This is core build-toward intelligence for the in-house chat roadmap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a 3-tier PPV ladder in the chatter SOP: Tier 1 (entry, $15–$20) → Tier 2 (mid, $30–$50) → Tier 3 (high-ticket, $80–$100+). Require at least 2 Tier 1 purchases before a Tier 3 offer. Encode this sequencing into AI chatting logic when that project advances.

findings.title: Always wrap up a sexting session before logging off to prevent churn

variants.claim: Abruptly ending a chat session after a subscriber has spent money makes them feel used and dramatically reduces the chance they return or spend again.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are explicitly instructed to close every sexting/monetization session with a proper conversational wrap-up — warm sign-off, leaving the subscriber feeling positive and wanting to return. Logging off mid-session is a fireable-level error in the VA training.

sources.quote: always wrap up the conversation sexting session before logging off this is to leave the subscriber feeling good and wanting to come back for more next time otherwise if you abruptly stop replying the subscriber has spent hundreds of dollars on content they're going to feel used

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is outsourced (§8) with no internal SOP. This 'session closure' practice is a key retention mechanic that should be codified in Palm's future in-house chatter SOP and AI chatting prompt instructions.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add 'session closure protocol' to the chatter SOP: before ending any active conversation, send a warm closing message (e.g. affirmation, teaser for next time). Make this a scored QA checkpoint when reviewing chatter performance.

findings.title: Send a single sad-face emoji to re-engage non-responding subscribers

variants.claim: Sending a lone sad-face emoji to a subscriber who has gone quiet triggers guilt and reliably provokes a response, re-opening the monetization window.
variants.what_they_do: When a subscriber has not replied to a previous message, instead of sending follow-up texts or more content pitches, the chatter sends only a '😢' or '🥺' emoji. Bjorn claims ~99.9% response rate. The mechanism: guilt-trips the subscriber into explaining their absence, which restarts rapport and spending. No spamming — one emoji only.

sources.quote: if a subscriber has not replied to your previous message and you want to get his attention simply just send a sad face emoji it is amazing how this will work I would say this probably works 99.9% of the time

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented re-engagement tactic (§8 outsourced, §9 partial fan retention). This is a high-signal, zero-cost tactic for the in-house chat roadmap and is also relevant to AI chatting logic (trigger sad emoji after N hours of non-response).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build this into the chatter re-engagement SOP: if subscriber has not replied after 24–48 hours, send a single sad-face emoji (no text). Log the response rate in Fan Tracker CRM to validate. For AI chatting, add as an automated trigger: no-reply after configurable timeout → send sad emoji → await response before any further action.

findings.title: Never send multiple follow-up messages to a non-responding subscriber

variants.claim: Sending multiple unanswered messages in a row appears overwhelming and spammy, actively pushing subscribers away and reducing future engagement.
variants.what_they_do: The VA training guide explicitly prohibits sending additional messages if a subscriber has not responded. One message sits unanswered → chatter holds. The only permitted follow-up is the sad-face emoji tactic (single message). This is a firm SOP rule, not a suggestion.

sources.quote: do not spam subscribers with messages if they're not responding try not to send additional messages if the sub is not responding as this can appear overwhelming and spammy and could potentially put the subscriber off

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no internal chat SOP (§8 outsourced). If the external team lacks this discipline it may be causing churn Palm can't see. This rule should be a core constraint in both the in-house chatter SOP and AI chatting prompt guardrails.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a hard rule to the chatter SOP and AI chatting system prompt: after one unanswered message, no further text messages until the subscriber replies (only the sad-face emoji exception is permitted). Make this a QA audit criterion when reviewing chatter logs.

findings.title: Replace rule-sets with 4–5 core values to govern chatter behavior

variants.claim: Values (4–5 core beliefs) outperform rule-sets because they apply to every situation, are easier to enforce, and create a positive work environment.
variants.what_they_do: Agency defines 4–5 values instead of an exhaustive rule list: (1) work for creators / act in their best interest even if it costs money; (2) everyone spends — treat every fan as a potential buyer; (3) make your opportunities — proactively go to fans; (4) play for the team — think agency-wide, not just personal earnings. When a chatter's decision is questioned, the manager asks 'how did that align with our values?' rather than citing a broken rule.

sources.quote: Four to five core beliefs that you want instilled in your chatting team

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's DM monetization is fully outsourced to an external chatting team (§8); no internal values framework or SOP exists. This is build-toward intelligence for the planned in-house chat team (~1yr out).
palm_comparison.recommendation: When building the in-house chat team, document 4–5 agency values as the governance layer before writing any procedural rules. Draft values now so they're ready for onboarding the first internal chatter.

findings.title: Daily end-of-shift written takeaways lock in incremental chatter learning

variants.claim: Requiring chatters to record written takeaways at the end of every shift — specifically one actionable improvement for the next shift — accelerates skill development during the first month.
variants.what_they_do: For roughly the first month, each chatter writes a brief end-of-shift note answering: 'What are the actionable steps I'll take next shift to improve on what I did this shift?' This creates a self-directed feedback loop between supervised live-training sessions.

sources.quote: For the first month or so, get the chatters to record their daily takeaways. At the end of each shift, okay, what are the actionable steps you're going to take next shift

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal chatter development loop at Palm (§8, outsourced). Build-toward.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Include a daily takeaway template in the chatter onboarding kit. A simple Airtable or Notion form ('shift date / one thing to improve / how I'll do it differently') submitted at shift-end gives managers a lightweight pulse without requiring constant live supervision.

findings.title: Start at 50/50 split as baseline; negotiate higher % as skills improve

variants.claim: 50/50 is the standard baseline commission split for new OFM agencies, with room to take a higher percentage as expertise and results grow.
variants.what_they_do: Agency locks in 50/50 as the minimum split with every creator. As the agency proves its value and techniques mature, it renegotiates upward. No tiered structure described — purely experience-based progression.

sources.quote: you say let's partner up, I'll take 50%, you take 50%, this is the benchmark, this is the baseline

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm sets commission % per-creator at onboarding (§7) but has no documented split rationale, tiers, or progression logic. The baseline number Palm uses is not written down. This is flagged as §7's #1 gap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Codify 50/50 as Palm's stated baseline in the onboarding contract and pitch deck. Document criteria for when Palm negotiates above 50% (e.g., creator already earning >$X/mo, Palm providing full chatting, paid traffic spend). Gives sales clarity and creator trust.

findings.title: Target creators who are plateaued, overwhelmed, or burned by a bad agency

variants.claim: The highest-converting creator prospects are those who are stuck at a plateau, overwhelmed by workload, or have had a bad agency experience — not cold beginners.
variants.what_they_do: Agency explicitly looks for creators who: (a) plateaued after initial growth, (b) are overwhelmed managing content + chatting alone, (c) had a bad prior agency and are open to a better partner. Uses these pain points as the outreach hook.

sources.quote: you're looking for models that are a bit overwhelmed, they're a bit stuck, their accounts are being plateaued, maybe they've had a bad agency before

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented ICP (ideal creator profile) or qualification criteria for outbound (§6). Josh-led DM outreach is ad hoc. Targeting burned/plateaued creators is a concrete, testable filter Palm could apply immediately.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define Palm's creator ICP in writing: earning $1K–$10K/mo, plateaued 60+ days, active IG but not growing, or recently left an agency. Use these as DM targeting filters on IG/TikTok. Add a qualification question to the onboarding form: 'Have you worked with an agency before? What happened?'

findings.title: Proactively coach creators on IG explicit-content ban risk to prevent account loss

variants.claim: Many creators post explicit content on IG out of ignorance, and the agency's value-add is intervening before the ban happens, not after.
variants.what_they_do: Agency flags explicit-content posting as a ban trigger and actively tells creators to stop. Positions this knowledge as a differentiator when pitching creators — 'I can protect your account' is part of the acquisition hook. General IG compliance coaching (what to post, how often, which platforms) is part of the agency's ongoing service.

sources.quote: some will do really explicit content on IG and we all know now that is not a thing you can do — if you do put this content up on IG your account's going to get banned

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content pipeline (§1) is data-driven for ideation but has no documented compliance coaching SOP for creators around IG content rules. Real-creator IG warmup/cadence is also flagged as a gap (§4 THIN for real creators).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'Platform Rules' module to Palm's creator onboarding: a short brief covering IG explicit-content policy, shadowban triggers, and safe posting formats. Deliver this in the first week. Also use IG compliance knowledge as a sales proof point in acquisition outreach ('we've kept X accounts safe').

findings.title: Run weekly payouts and performance check-ins as a retention and motivation tool

variants.claim: Weekly payouts ('weekly pouch') keep both the agency and creator motivated and signal operational professionalism early in the relationship.
variants.what_they_do: After onboarding, agency moves to a weekly payout cadence. Framed as both a financial rhythm and a morale tool — seeing money arrive weekly reinforces creator buy-in and agency credibility. No detailed reporting format described, but the weekly cadence is presented as standard practice.

sources.quote: after a week or two, we do weekly pouch, just to let you know — that's just a cool little tip, enjoy the money coming in

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm auto-computes weekly invoices (§7) so the cadence matches. However, Palm has no documented creator-facing performance review or check-in tied to the payout — it's finance-only. No retention/success cadence is written down (§9 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Attach a lightweight weekly creator report to Palm's existing invoice email: top 3 metrics (subscribers, revenue, top-performing post). Takes minimal effort given Palm's Airtable data and Resend pipeline, and transforms a finance touchpoint into a retention and upsell moment.

findings.topic: Price integrity / repackaging instead of discounting
findings.title: Never fold on PPV price — repackage content instead of discounting

variants.claim: When a fan doesn't purchase at the stated PPV price, cutting the price cheapens the brand; instead, trim or repackage the same video at a lower price point so it appears to be different content.
variants.what_they_do: If a fan ignores or declines a PPV, the chatter does not reduce the listed price. Instead, they clip the same video to a different length, change whether a thumbnail is shown, or rebundle it, then re-pitch it as a 'different video you might like' at a lower price. This preserves the perceived value of the original piece.

sources.quote: if there's a point where they don't purchase don't fold on your pricing what you can do though is you can trim videos make them different lengths repackage it so it doesn't seem like you are folding

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal PPV pricing model or price-integrity policy is documented at Palm (§7 GAP). This is a concrete tactic for the in-house chat and pricing buildout.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'no-discount, repackage only' rule to the future chat team SOP. Build a small asset library of clip variants (different lengths, thumbnail on/off) for each PPV set so chatters have ready-made repackaged versions to offer.

findings.topic: Revenue split between subscriptions vs. DMs
findings.title: Benchmark: most OF revenue comes from messages, not subscriptions

variants.claim: The vast majority of a high-performing creator's OF income comes from DM/PPV revenue, not subscription fees — illustrated by a creator earning $68K from subs vs. $300K+ from messages.
variants.what_they_do: Black Cherry uses this ratio as a core KPI framing: subscriptions are the door, DM monetization is the business. They orient their entire agency operation (chatters, scripts, PPV strategy) around maximising message revenue rather than subscriber count.

sources.quote: only made 68 000 over 300 000 literally from the subscriptions the only fan subscriptions and most of the money as you can see came from the messages themselves

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's invoicing system tracks total creator revenue and commissions (§7) but no documented KPI split between subscription vs. message revenue exists. Palm's content engine (§1) is optimised for top-of-funnel subscriber acquisition; the DM monetisation side is outsourced with limited visibility (§8).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a sub-vs-message revenue split metric to Palm's invoicing dashboard and whale-hunting reports (§9). Use this ratio as a health KPI per creator: if message revenue is <50% of total, the chatting strategy is underperforming. Share this benchmark with the external chat team as an accountability target.

findings.title: Hierarchical folder labeling system for OF vault content reuse

variants.claim: Organizing vault content by year/quarter/month/week with descriptive labels enables fast retrieval and reselling to new subscribers.
variants.what_they_do: Creator maintains a Dropbox folder tree (Year → Q1-Q4 → Month → Week → Day), names individual files descriptively (e.g. 'pink outfit 1', 'pink outfit 2'), and mirrors this structure inside the OF vault using OF's native label/folder feature. Only edited, reusable content is saved. Legacy content is batched into a single 'pre-2022' folder and backfilled gradually rather than all at once.

sources.quote: I took everything from before 2022 and I put that in one folder and now I created a new folder and it is called of content 2023 and from there I have q1 2 3 and 4

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content system (§2) is THIN on OF vault/PPV organization. The social pipeline (§1) is mature for top-of-funnel assets but there is no documented system for tagging, retrieving, or reselling vault content. This is a direct gap in the OF vault layer.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a vault taxonomy standard for Palm-managed creators: Year → Month → content-type label (e.g. '2024-03-booty', '2024-03-face'). Add a vault-labeling step to the existing editor workflow so every uploaded asset is tagged before the task is closed. Build a lightweight 'resell menu' Airtable view that surfaces labeled vault assets by creator for chatter/PPV use.

findings.title: Pin a pre-made content menu to OF page to drive passive vault resales

variants.claim: Pinning a priced menu of previously sent content to the OF page lets new subscribers self-serve old vault content without creator intervention.
variants.what_they_do: Creator builds a text-based menu listing past content items with descriptions and prices, pins it to their OF page. New subscribers browse it and request items by name; creator pulls from the vault folder and sends. No active selling required — the menu does the work.

sources.quote: I also created another menu that has previously sent out content so I've just organized it I label what it is and the price and then I've also pinned that to my page

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented equivalent for a passive vault-resell menu (§2 gap). The chatting function is outsourced (§8), so the external chat team likely has no visibility into a structured content menu either. This is a low-effort monetization layer currently missing.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Standardize a pinned content menu for each Palm creator. The editor or ops team maintains it in Airtable (listing vault item name, label, price, thumbnail). Brief the external chat team to reference this menu when fans ask about past content. When the in-house chat team is built (~1yr roadmap), make menu-referencing a core chat SOP.

findings.title: Length-based PPV pricing ladder: $5–$8 short, $8–$20 long, nothing under 1 min

variants.claim: Pricing PPV content by video length with a hard floor of 1 minute and a practical ceiling of ~$20 maximizes purchase rate across different fan spending levels.
variants.what_they_do: Short or clothed videos: under $8. Longer or more explicit videos: $8–$20. Hard rule: never charge for content under 1 minute — that goes free. Photos posted free to subscribers (subscription covers them); photos only charged if custom. Sends a mix of price points so lower-budget fans still convert on cheaper items while high-spenders can hit the $20–$25 ceiling.

sources.quote: if I send out a shorter video or like a twerking video or I'm clothed then it's somewhere under like eight dollars and if it's a little bit more than that then it's somewhere over eight dollars always under I would say twenty dollars

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented PPV pricing model (§7 — #1 gap flagged). No internal baseline exists for price ladders, anchoring, or length-based rules. This is pure learn-from-others territory.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt a default PPV pricing framework for all Palm creators: <1 min = free/engagement only; 1–3 min = $5–$8; 3–8 min = $8–$15; 8+ min = $15–$25. Document as a pricing SOP and share with the external chat team so they apply consistent pricing in DMs. Review per-creator after 30 days and adjust based on conversion data already in the system.

findings.title: Send at least one PPV mass message per day — treat it like TV advertising repetition

variants.claim: Sending PPV messages at minimum once daily, using a TV-advertising repetition mindset, is the single biggest lever for DM revenue most creators underuse.
variants.what_they_do: Creator sends PPV mass messages at least once per day, sometimes multiple times. Frames it explicitly as a repetition/awareness play (Tide detergent analogy): fans who don't buy message #1 may buy message #5. Explicitly rejects the 'one message per week' approach. Pairs this with multiple free posts per day on the OF feed to maintain presence.

sources.quote: you should be sending out pay-per-view messages at least once a day if you're not you are probably missing out on a lot of money

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is outsourced (§8) and there is no documented PPV cadence SOP. This is build-toward intelligence for both the in-house chat team (~1yr roadmap) and the AI chatting project. The daily-minimum rule is a concrete, codifiable KPI.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a PPV send-frequency KPI to the external chat team's performance brief immediately: minimum 1 mass PPV per creator per day. When building the in-house chat SOP (roadmap), hard-code this as a daily task in the chatter checklist. For the AI chatting project, model automated PPV scheduling at 1× daily with A/B price variation.

findings.title: Batch shoot 5 looks → batch schedule 5 days of page posts + paired PPV messages

variants.claim: Batching a multi-look shoot and scheduling all resulting page posts and PPV messages in one session prevents daily scramble and ensures consistent output.
variants.what_they_do: In a single shoot session: 5 outfits/looks, each producing ~3 photos + 2 videos. Immediately after editing, schedules each outfit's photos to post on consecutive days AND schedules the corresponding PPV video message to go out on the same day as those photos. Result: 5 days of content planned in one sitting. Uses OF's native calendar scheduler.

sources.quote: say I did five different looks today I went and did my photo shoot five different looks each look I took three photos and two videos so then what I would do is I would schedule you know tomorrow these photos the next day these photos

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's social content pipeline (§1) is strong on ideation and editing but posting cadence for real creators is not codified (§1 🔲 GAP). The OF vault/PPV side (§2) has no batch-scheduling SOP. This tactic bridges both gaps with a concrete daily-output model.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a batch content session standard for Palm real creators: minimum 3 looks per session, each look producing 2–3 photos (page) + 1 video (PPV). Ops team schedules page posts and paired PPV messages together in one Publer/OF scheduling pass. Add a 'batch session' record type to the Airtable Content Ops base to track sessions, looks, scheduled dates, and PPV prices per asset.

findings.topic: subscriber list segmentation for PPV targeting
findings.title: Segment subscriber lists by content preference and renewal status to send targeted PPV messages

variants.claim: Using OF's native list feature to segment fans by content preference (feet, striptease, etc.) and renewal status enables personalized PPV sends that feel direct and increase conversion.
variants.what_they_do: Creates named lists inside OF (beyond defaults): VIP list for high-paying subscribers ($200/mo tier who get PPV free), preference-based lists (feet fans, striptease fans, etc.). When sending a PPV mass message, selects the relevant list as the target or excludes VIP list from paid messages. Sends preference-matched messages to niche lists with personalized copy to increase perceived intimacy.

sources.quote: you can also create lists for certain people like if you know there are certain people that really like feet pictures or there's certain group of people that really like striptease videos you can add them to that list and then whenever you do want to send out a certain type of message you can send it to everybody

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's fan intelligence layer (§9) has a Fan Tracker CRM and whale-hunting module but no documented PPV targeting by content preference. Chatting is outsourced (§8) so the external team may not be segmenting sends. This is build-toward for both the in-house chat team and the AI chatting project.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Brief the external chat team to begin using OF list segmentation immediately: at minimum, maintain a 'VIP/renewing' list excluded from paid PPV, and build 2–3 preference lists per creator based on DM history. Feed preference data back into the Fan Tracker CRM (§9). When building the in-house chat SOP, make list maintenance a weekly chatter task. For the AI chatting project, model preference tagging as a fan-profile attribute driving automated PPV selection.

findings.topic: objection and boundary handling in DMs
findings.title: Two-warning boundary script: redirect → stern warning → block to protect creator brand

variants.claim: A scripted two-step response to out-of-scope requests (redirect to alternative, then firm final warning before block) maintains creator boundaries without losing a potential buyer.
variants.what_they_do: Step 1: When fan requests something creator doesn't offer, redirect: 'I don't do that but I do [X] — can I get you a custom video?' Step 2: If fan is pushy after first redirect, deliver a firm second message: 'I've told you once, I'll tell you twice — I'll block you if you continue.' Step 3: Block. Never debates or apologizes. Frames the redirect as a sales opportunity, not a refusal.

sources.quote: if someone's asking for something that I don't do I just say hey I don't actually do that but I do blank if you want to get a custom video or a custom picture whatever it is and I kind of offer them an alternative

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is outsourced (§8) with no internal scripts or objection-handling SOPs. This is concrete build-toward material for the in-house chat SOP. The redirect-to-upsell framing is especially valuable — it turns a boundary scenario into a custom content sale.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add this two-step boundary script to the future in-house chat team's SOP as a default response template. Share with the external chat team now as a briefing document. For the AI chatting project, model this as a conditional response tree: out-of-scope request → redirect + custom upsell offer → if repeated → block flag for human review.

findings.title: Six-step ad script for qualified creator leads

variants.claim: A repeatable six-step ad script (attention, key benefit, without-the-pain, proof, time-sensitive offer, downside of inaction) structures creative for conversion rather than ad-hoc messaging.
variants.what_they_do: Uses a fixed 6-step structure with one chosen angle (speed, risk-reduction, or ease); language must be coherent from ad to VSL to funnel to call and matched to a specific avatar (e.g. creators already doing 10k/mo) to avoid attracting low-quality opportunity-seekers.

sources.quote: the six-step ad script looks like this... number one, attention... number six is the downside of inaction

palm_comparison.vs_us: No acquisition messaging framework or avatar definition exists at Palm (§6 THIN).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt the 6-step script and define a concrete creator avatar (revenue band, follower count) so Palm outreach and any future content speaks one coherent, qualifying message.

findings.title: Real creator + AI clone, kept on OnlyFans

variants.claim: Cloning a real signed creator into an AI model and mixing AI with real content lets an agency keep OnlyFans conversion while multiplying volume.
variants.what_they_do: Take 20-30 photos of a real signed creator, train an AI model on her face/body, generate motion-control videos, then post a real+AI mix that all funnels to her real OnlyFans (no AI sold past the wall). One model films 35-40 videos/week; AI fills the other 40-45, enabling 4+ accounts.

sources.quote: you take a real creator and you generate your own AI profile of that creator

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm already runs a recreation pipeline for AI personas funneling to OnlyFans (§3), but does NOT yet clone REAL creators to scale their social volume. This is a direct extension of Palm's existing AI-persona capability onto the real-creator core business (§0/§1).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Pilot cloning one cooperative real creator: train an AI likeness, post a real/AI mix to her existing funnel, keep all OF vault content 100% real. Leverage Palm's existing recreation pipeline.

findings.title: Hybrid keeps OnlyFans trust vs full-AI Fanvue conversion loss

variants.claim: Full-AI creators are forced onto less-trusted third-party platforms where conversion drops 30-50% versus OnlyFans, making hybrid (real OF) structurally more profitable.
variants.what_they_do: Argues full AI cannot use OnlyFans (ID verification, no AI content allowed) so they go to Fansly/Fanvue/Fanfix where saved-credit-card trust is absent and conversion tanks; hybrid keeps everything on OnlyFans.

sources.quote: Your conversion rates will literally drop 30 to 50% just by doing that platform rather than Only Fans

palm_comparison.vs_us: Confirms Palm's core OnlyFans funnel as the high-conversion asset (§0). Palm's AI personas use OnlyFans-via-social, so this validates Palm's choice not to go Fanvue-only.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Hold the OnlyFans funnel as the conversion anchor for both real and AI lines; treat Fanvue-style platforms only as fallback, never primary.

findings.title: 20% real / 80% AI mix to keep trust and volume

variants.claim: A roughly 20% real and 80% AI content mix maximizes posting volume while keeping enough real proof that viewers trust the creator is a real person.
variants.what_they_do: Posts ~20% real creator content and ~80% AI; warns that 90-95% AI makes fans think the page is fake, while pin posts plus a recent real story verify authenticity. Recommends 30-50% real in week one to build trust faster.

sources.quote: 20% real content kept people trusting it

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a mature data-driven social pipeline (§1) but no codified real-vs-AI blend ratio for a hybrid creator. This gives a concrete starting ratio.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt a default ~20-30% real / 70-80% AI blend for hybrid creators, front-loading real content during launch; codify the ratio in the SMM content spec.

findings.title: Strip AI metadata before posting or reach is throttled

variants.claim: AI-generated media carries hidden metadata that platforms read to tag/throttle content, so removing it before posting materially restores reach.
variants.what_they_do: Removes AI metadata from generated images/videos with a free tool or re-export before posting; reports reach went up ~40% and videos hit explore again after they started doing this.

sources.quote: there's a metadata attached to those files that says AI

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's AI-persona program is built for compliance (EU AI Act label + watermark, §3), which is the opposite posture. This is a competitor risk tactic Palm should note but weigh against its compliance stance.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Note as a reach lever for non-EU/unlabeled experiments only; do NOT apply to Palm's EU-AI-Act-labeled personas. Flag the compliance tension for leadership.

findings.title: Motion control lets you copy trends in minutes

variants.claim: Generating AI versions of trending videos via motion control lets an agency post a trend within minutes instead of waiting days for a creator to film.
variants.what_they_do: Downloads a trending reference video, feeds it plus the AI model image into Cling motion control, and produces the creator's version of the trend in ~5-10 minutes to ride the wave while it is still hot.

sources.quote: see a trend, generate it

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's inspo pipeline (§1) identifies WHAT to film from viral data but still depends on the creator filming. Motion control collapses the produce-time gap Palm currently has.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a motion-control fast-path to the inspo pipeline so promoted trend reels can be AI-rendered for hybrid creators within the trend window.

findings.title: Main brand account fed by disposable burner accounts

variants.claim: On X, a protected main brand account funneled by several disposable high-volume burner accounts maximizes growth while insulating the asset from bans.
variants.what_they_do: Runs one main account (2-3 quality posts/day, funnels to OF) plus 3-5 burner accounts (3-4 aggressive trend posts/day) that spam trends and pin replies funneling all traffic to the main; if a burner is banned, it's disposable.

sources.quote: These are your growth engine

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented traffic/distribution strategy for X (§5 gap). This is a concrete account-architecture pattern Palm lacks entirely.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Pilot a main+burner X structure for one creator; treat burners as expendable growth engines and the main as the OF-converting asset.

findings.title: Engagement-trap, trendjack, and paid promo pages on X

variants.claim: X growth is driven by reply-baiting engagement traps, fast trendjacking, and paying large promo pages to seed content with mystery hooks.
variants.what_they_do: Posts reply-bait ('describe me in one word'), jumps trends like Grok/Epstein image gimmicks within 15-30 min, and pays promo pages (100-500k followers) $50-200/post that hook viewers into clicking through to the creator's pinned reply.

sources.quote: the content designed to get replies

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's traffic strategy is undocumented (§5 gap). These are specific paid + organic X tactics with no Palm baseline.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Capture as build-out menu for an X traffic SOP: engagement-trap caption library, daily trend monitoring, and a tested promo-page budget per creator.

findings.title: Girlfriend-experience parasocial selling, not PPV spam

variants.claim: The girlfriend experience (parasocial connection, scripted live-feeling sells, no PPV spam) drives the highest and most durable chatting revenue.
variants.what_they_do: Optimizes the OF wall to feel like 'Snapchatting,' uses 7-10 stage scripts with verification images, never initiates sexual content (lets the fan initiate), handles broke fans with ego-protecting lines, and forbids PPV spamming across the whole team.

sources.quote: the GF experience, the only thing that I try to run in OFM

palm_comparison.vs_us: Chatting is OUTSOURCED at Palm with no in-house SOP (§8). This is direct build-toward intelligence for Palm's planned in-house and AI chat teams.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Capture the GFE framework (wall optimization, staged scripts, verification, no-spam rule) as the seed SOP for Palm's future in-house/AI chatting.

findings.title: Track chatting ratio; target 1:9-1:10

variants.claim: Chatting performance should be measured as a ratio of message/tip revenue to subscription revenue, with elite teams targeting roughly 1:9 to 1:10.
variants.what_they_do: Calculates (messages + tips) / subscriptions; reports turning a ~1:1 ratio creator into 1:10+, and uses the ratio gap on creator calls to show lost revenue (e.g. a 1:3 vs 1:9 difference of thousands/month).

sources.quote: add up your messages and tips as well and divide it by your subscriptions

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm collects fan/whale data (§9) but has no documented chatting KPI like this. The ratio is a clean leadership metric Palm could adopt immediately even while chatting is outsourced.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Have the outsourced chat team report a weekly chatting ratio per creator now, and bake the metric into Palm's whale-hunting/Chat Team Report views.

findings.title: Three chatters + reliever for seamless 24/7 shifts

variants.claim: Per-creator chatting is staffed with three eight-hour chatters plus one reliever, with documented handoffs for seamless 24/7 coverage.
variants.what_they_do: Runs 3 chatters (six days) + 1 reliever (three days), all 8-hour shifts; Discord clock-in/out, end-of-shift reports, and at-next-chatter handoff messages so in-progress conversations transfer without gaps.

sources.quote: Three chatters and one reliever

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm outsources chatting and has no internal shift model (§8). This is a concrete staffing template for the future in-house team.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt the 3+1 eight-hour shift model with Discord clock-in/EOS reports as the staffing blueprint when Palm builds its in-house chat team.

findings.title: Revenue-gated hiring ladder: VAs, then chat manager, then ops

variants.claim: Hires should be added on a revenue-gated ladder — owner does everything to ~5k, VAs/chatters at 5-20k, managers at 20k+, ops director at 50-100k+.
variants.what_they_do: 0-5k owner solo; 5-20k first VA/chatter (after chatting yourself 2 weeks); 20-40k chat manager first then content/account managers; 50-100k+ ops director to execute proven systems so the owner exits day-to-day.

sources.quote: you need to hire managers

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's org is thin with no documented hiring criteria, comp bands, or scaling plan (§10). This is a ready-made staffing/comp ladder.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Use this ladder as a template for Palm's org-scaling plan; map Palm roles (Amin, editors, chat) onto the stages and define revenue gates for next hires.

findings.title: Loom-video SOPs to eliminate training misinterpretation

variants.claim: Recording yourself doing each task as a Loom video (rather than written docs) is the most reliable way to train hires and remove misinterpretation.
variants.what_they_do: Films a Loom once per task and reuses it forever; pairs Loom with backup documents for objections; uses live chatter monitoring on first 1-2 shifts plus 'trust but verify' checks for the first 1-2 weeks.

sources.quote: Loom recordings

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has SOP-style workflows in the editor/warmup UIs but no documented Loom-first training standard for new hires (§10). Cheap, high-leverage practice.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Standardize Loom-video SOPs for every Palm role (editor, poster, warmup operator, future chatters); store alongside the existing playbook editor.

findings.title: Bans driven by unbranded sexual content, not IP/proxies

variants.claim: Instagram bans in OFM are driven mainly by unfocused overtly-sexual content and low trust score, not by IP/proxy/link setup as most operators assume.
variants.what_they_do: After testing 50+ accounts, concludes content (half-naked, unbranded, unfocused) is the highest-weight ban trigger; uses normal iPhone + real SIM/hotspot, no VPN/proxy, and builds a branded persona instead of bikini spam.

sources.quote: 90% of bans initiate due to your content having zero focus

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's AI warmup is hardware-isolated with proxies/device separation (§3/§4); for REAL creators Palm has no documented IG ban-avoidance posture (§4 gap). This reframes the real-creator risk as content-led.
palm_comparison.recommendation: For real-creator IG, prioritize branded non-explicit content and normal-user behavior over technical spoofing; document a real-creator ban-avoidance SOP.

findings.title: Branded persona replacing pure volume in 2026

variants.claim: A distinct creator persona (identity, consistent theme, story) is replacing raw posting volume as the driver of growth, conversion, and ban-resistance.
variants.what_they_do: Defines a brand as persona + consistent theme + story (e.g. dominant/funny/confident); reports a branded creator going 2k to 50k/mo in two months, and writes a one-sentence persona applied to bios, captions, content, and chats.

sources.quote: make it that her persona

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's pipeline optimizes for viral content ideation (§1) but persona/branding is not a codified input. This argues brand should be a first-class field upstream of content selection.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a defined persona/brand brief to creator onboarding and feed it into the inspo pipeline so promoted ideas reinforce a consistent identity.

findings.title: Hybrid AI makes salary creators highly profitable

variants.claim: Pairing a low-cost salary creator with AI volume yields outsized margins because output is decoupled from the creator's motivation or filming.
variants.what_they_do: Cites a student paying salary creators ~$1.8-2k/mo each, generating ~$15-20k/mo per creator via hybrid AI volume, keeping the bulk as profit since the salary model only needs to produce NSFW vault content.

sources.quote: he's paying 6K to make over $45,000

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm sets commission % per creator at onboarding but has no documented pricing/commission MODEL or salary-vs-split rationale (§7 gap). This is a concrete alternative deal structure.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Evaluate a salary-creator + AI-volume deal structure as an alternative to revenue split for select creators; document the margin math in a pricing model.

findings.title: Snapchat as an under-saturated OF traffic + income channel

variants.claim: Snapchat is an under-exploited OFM channel that simultaneously drives OF subs, cross-promotes other platforms, and pays direct creator monetization.
variants.what_they_do: Sets up a real-looking account, posts ~5 spotlights/day plus 30-100 clickbait story snaps/day (repurposed via repurpose.io or a VA), CTAs only once a week to avoid disabling, and uses paid promoted-snap boosts; cites a creator earning as much from Snapchat as from Instagram.

sources.quote: boost your initial following

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's distribution is IG/TikTok via Publer + Telegram (§5); Snapchat is absent from Palm's stack. New channel with no Palm baseline.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Pilot Snapchat for one high-output creator: repurpose existing content, weekly-only CTA, test promoted snaps; measure as a third revenue lever.

findings.title: Central account/team/finance trackers to remove owner from ops

variants.claim: A scaled agency runs on connected central databases (accounts, team, content, finance) so operational detail lives in systems, not the owner's head.
variants.what_they_do: Runs six linked Notion systems (model account manager with logins/warmup SOPs, VA/team tracker with pay+KPIs, content HQ, finance tracker, iPhone tracker, AI to-do list) that interlink so the owner only reviews and decides.

sources.quote: the model account manager

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm is already strong here — Airtable HQ + Content Ops, editor task queue, invoicing, whale CRM (§1/§7/§9). Validates Palm's systemization; the gap is a unified creator-phase/KPI rollup.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a single creator-phase + KPI rollup (revenue, chatting ratio, follower growth, account status) across Palm's Airtable bases, surfaced in the SMM hub Overview.

findings.title: Keep the profile wall teasing, never explicit, to preserve PPV value

variants.claim: Profile/wall content should tease (lingerie, not nudity) so curiosity—and willingness to pay for PPV—stays intact.
variants.what_they_do: Refuses to put nudes or explicit content on the OF wall, reasoning that if the fan sees everything for free or at subscription price there is nothing left to sell; keeps the wall at lingerie-level to sustain curiosity.

sources.quote: do not put any nudes... why is the fan going to spend any money when he's basically seen all of that for free

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm is strong on social top-of-funnel content (§1 MATURE) but thin on OF-vault/PPV content strategy (§2 THIN); this is a concrete vault-merchandising rule.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt a 'tease-not-reveal on the wall' rule as part of Palm's OF-vault/PPV content guidance.

findings.title: Win back expired subscribers with a discounted mass message on day one

variants.claim: Immediately re-activate a new creator's expired/lapsed subscribers with a discounted or trial-link mass message before chasing new traffic.
variants.what_they_do: On signing a creator, mass-message her expired subs (often already in the following list via auto-follow) with a trial/discount link; expect a spike of returning subs in the first week. Framed as the easy first win because retaining/reactivating is cheaper than finding new viral reels.

sources.quote: send out a mass message to all of her existing expired subscribers... put together a trial link or a discount link... you should see a big spike of these expired subs coming back.

palm_comparison.vs_us: No documented win-back/reactivation routine (§9 GAP); chatting outsourced (§8), so this is build-toward intelligence.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build-toward: make a day-one expired-subs win-back blast a standard onboarding step in the in-house/AI chat workflow.

findings.title: Manage creator expectations week-by-week or they invent their own

variants.claim: Explicitly set what will happen in week 1, month 1, and month 2 so the creator doesn't form unrealistic expectations and churn.
variants.what_they_do: On the onboarding call, walk through the timeline for the first week and first two months. Rationale: 'if you don't manage people's expectations, that's when they make their own,' which are usually unrealistic and lead to early departure when results don't match.

sources.quote: if you don't manage people's expectations, that's when they make their own expectations, which typically is not really reasonable... they leave.

palm_comparison.vs_us: No documented expectation-setting framework in onboarding (§6 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Bake a week-1/month-1/month-2 expectation script into the onboarding call to reduce early churn.

findings.title: Gate chatter hires on English, typing speed, and connection before training

variants.claim: Screen chatters on measurable competencies before hiring and training, since bad chatting silently destroys revenue.
variants.what_they_do: Before hiring a chatter, run an English skill test, a Wi-Fi speed test, and a words-per-minute test; only those who pass get hired and trained. Bad chatters (spelling/grammar errors, mispriced first PPVs like $4.99 or $200) are a top-7 agency-killing mistake.

sources.quote: we give them an English skill test... a Wi-Fi speed test... a words per minute test. Only once they check out on all of these, that is when you hire them.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Chatting is outsourced (§8) so Palm doesn't run this screen today; it's build-toward intelligence for the planned in-house chat team's hiring SOP.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build-toward: when standing up the in-house chat team, adopt a pre-hire screen (English / WPM / connection) and a first-PPV pricing standard.

findings.title: Standardized weekly content box per model

variants.claim: Standardizing creator output into a fixed weekly content format lets an agency scale production across many models with minimal model and founder time.
variants.what_they_do: Hive Media (faceless francis interview) requires two 'sets' per model per week, each composed of part A casual SFW realism content, part B sexy social content, and part C a sexting set or PPV, so every model drops into the same box that feeds both the marketing and the sales machines on four-to-six hours of model time.

sources.quote: every week the models have to deliver two sets. A set consists of A, B, and C

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a mature, data-driven SOCIAL ideation pipeline (§1) but no standardized OF vault/PPV content format (§2 THIN). Partial gap on type-2 content.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a standard weekly content-set format for Palm creators (SFW realism / sexy social / vault-or-PPV) so vault production scales the same way the social pipeline already does.

findings.title: Spreadsheet/Airtable backend at scale

variants.claim: Running account and content operations off spreadsheet-style databases with front-end interfaces is the pragmatic backbone for managing accounts at scale.
variants.what_they_do: Hive Media stores 10,000+ social accounts (proxy, 2FA, status, creation date) in Excel/Sheets as the database with front-end interfaces on top, updated automatically by bots, and explicitly says Airtable is just a prettier version of the same database approach.

sources.quote: the only thing better than a spreadsheet might be a slightly prettier spreadsheet in something like Air Table

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm already runs on Airtable (HQ + Content Ops bases) with a Next.js admin front end (§0), so Palm is aligned with this best practice. Validation, not a gap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Keep the Airtable-as-database plus admin-UI pattern; ensure account-status fields (proxy, ban status, creation date) are tracked and auto-updated as rigorously as Hive does.

findings.title: Provision reserve accounts for burn

variants.claim: Assuming a baseline account-ban burn rate and pre-provisioning reserve accounts is required to run social acquisition at scale without interruption.
variants.what_they_do: Hive Media issues 200 accounts per platform per model (about 100 active immediately, 100 in reserve), runs a daily script using public profile views to auto-detect bans, and accepts that even fully organic operations always carry some burn.

sources.quote: 100 that become active immediately when they start then 100 for reserve... there's always the risk of having some bans

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm runs hardware-isolated AI account warmup (§3/§4 MATURE) but has no documented reserve-account or burn-rate provisioning model. Gap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt a reserve-account buffer per AI persona plus an automated daily ban-detection check, sized to an expected burn rate, so a ban never stalls a creator's funnel.

findings.title: Build so the founder can disappear

variants.claim: Heavy supervision layers and clear workflows let the founder step away while the business keeps running, which is the real test of a scaled agency.
variants.what_they_do: Hive Media (300 employees, 60 models) layers supervisors into category managers into shift/market managers per department, and the founder says if he disappeared for six months the company would still run profitably.

sources.quote: if I disappear for 6 months right now, will my company grow? Maybe not exponentially, but it will run

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a thin org with many decisions through the founder and no org chart or scaling plan (§10 THIN). Direct gap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a supervision layer (team leads / managers) per Palm function with explicit workflows so the founder can step out without the operation stalling.

findings.title: AI scores chat transcripts daily

variants.claim: An AI model trained on past chats can read every chatter's transcripts daily and surface buy-signals, missed offers, and coaching feedback that human supervisors cannot cover at scale.
variants.what_they_do: Hive Media pulls Infloww message-board exports into an LLM trained on two years of their own chats to produce daily per-chatter reports (interest/buy signals vs offers sent, left-on-read counts, missed opportunities, KPIs vs peers) because even five supervisors cannot watch 30-40 chatters live.

sources.quote: an AI LLM model that we train... based on two years worth of data of our own chats... how many interest signals... opportunities they missed

palm_comparison.vs_us: Chatting is outsourced and Palm owns no QA loop (§8 OUTSOURCED), but AI chatting is an active Palm project. Direct build-toward for that roadmap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: For Palm's AI-chatting project, design an AI transcript-QA layer that scores every conversation daily (buy-signal capture, offers sent, misses) and feeds coaching back to chatters.

findings.title: Give every model a written character

variants.claim: Building a written character/persona for each creator and aligning all content to it raises LTV by enabling a parasocial relationship rather than generic thirst traps.
variants.what_they_do: Hive Media sits each new model down to create an in-depth character sheet (occupation, personality, persona, backstory) and aligns outfits, scenes, and PPVs to it, deliberately targeting spending demographics by the model's look so chatters can monetize a fleshed-out character.

sources.quote: you're actually a character with a story and depth... build a better parasocial relationship with them

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's social pipeline drives content from viral inspo data (§1) but there is no documented per-creator character/persona layer tying content to a sales story. Gap on branding/type-2.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a per-creator character sheet (persona, backstory, target fan demographic) that both content and the chat team work from, so content ladders into monetization.

findings.title: Ladder PPV prices upward in sequence

variants.claim: Selling via an escalating price ladder rather than one high ask raises total spend while keeping each step psychologically easy.
variants.what_they_do: Luca Pritchard runs a default ladder of 15, 25, 45, 65, then 100-120, and warns that hitting a warmed-up fan with a single $80 PPV makes him leave for no money, where the same total is extracted reliably in small rising steps.

sources.quote: We'll start at 15 we'll then go to 25 we'll then go to 45 we'll then go to 65 and... 100 or 120

palm_comparison.vs_us: No formal price ladder at Palm (§7 THIN). Direct gap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Publish a standard Palm PPV ladder with rising steps and trigger conditions, instead of single high asks.
